title: Incubating hatching eggs
subtitle: A three-week guide from setting eggs to hatch day.
excerpt: An incubator gives you control over hatching outside the traditional broody season. You choose the breed, you choose the timing, and you handle the variables that a broody hen manages instinctively. The margin for error is thin but the process is straightforward once you understand what the egg needs at each stage.
sourceNotes: DEFRA, Code of Practice for the Welfare of Laying Hens and Chickens (2018). Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive, chapter on artificial incubation.

content.text: A broody hen maintains temperature, humidity, and turning automatically. An incubator requires you to monitor all three. The three-week arc is consistent across all chicken breeds; the key variables are the first candling at day 7 to identify infertile eggs, the second at day 14 to check air cell development, and the
at day 18 when you stop turning and raise the humidity.
Sourcing and storing eggs before setting

Hatching eggs from your own flock need a cockerel that has been running with the hens for at least a week; he will have fertilised the eggs held in lay over that period. Eggs can be stored pointed-end down at room temperature (15 to 18°C) for up to 10 days before incubation without a significant drop in hatchability; after 14 days hatchability falls sharply. Do not refrigerate them.

Days 1 to 18: 37.5°C, 45 to 55% relative humidity. Days 19 to 21 (lockdown): 37.5°C, 65 to 70% relative humidity. A forced-air incubator with a built-in humidity reader will monitor both. In a still-air incubator without a fan, measure temperature at the top of the egg rather than at the base of the incubator; still-air machines run hotter at the top.

Turn eggs a minimum of 3 times daily up to day 18, always an odd number of turns per day so the egg finishes each night on the opposite side from where it started. An automatic turner built into the incubator handles this; if turning by hand, mark each egg with an X on one side so you can track which way it has been turned.
At day 7, remove each egg from the incubator, hold it point-down against a torch in a darkened room, and look through the shell.

takes less than a minute per egg. Work quickly; the egg should not be out of the incubator for more than 10 minutes total. Infertile eggs show as completely clear; remove them to prevent any burst egg contaminating the incubator.
The first sign of hatch is a

: a small crack or hole in the shell near the broad end. The chick then works around the circumference of the shell, cutting with its egg tooth. The process from first pip to fully out of the shell takes 12 to 24 hours. Do not open the incubator during hatching; the drop in humidity is the most common cause of chicks becoming shrink-wrapped in the shell.

entries.cue: No eggs showing development at day 7 candling
entries.fix: Remove all clear eggs and check the cockerel's mating behaviour before the next batch. A fresh batch of eggs from a proven pair is the most reliable remedy.
entries.cause: Either the cockerel is infertile, has not had access to the hens long enough, or the eggs were stored too long or at the wrong temperature before setting

entries.cue: Chicks pipping but not progressing after 24 hours
entries.fix: Increase incubator humidity to 75% and wait a further 12 hours before considering assisted hatching. Assisted hatching carries real risk; leave it as a last resort.
entries.cause: Low humidity has caused the membrane to dry and shrink around the chick; the chick cannot turn

entries.fix: Move the incubator away from windows and doors. Check the seal on the viewing window. If the thermostat is failing, the hatch rate will be poor but eggs already developing are worth continuing.
entries.cause: Draft in the room, lid not fully sealed, or faulty thermostat
